statement.markdown: Given a linked list, you need to flatten it into a single-level linked list. A linked list is considered flattened if it has no nodes with two or more children (i.e., a node can have at most one right child). The original order of nodes must be preserved.
statement.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer representing the number of nodes in the linked list. Each node is represented by a line containing three integers: the node's value, the left child node index, and the right child node index. The node indices are 0-based and -1 represents a null child.
statement.outputFormat: The first line contains an integer representing the number of nodes in the flattened linked list. Each subsequent line contains a single integer representing the value of a node in the flattened linked list.

constraints.description: The number of nodes in the linked list.
The values of the nodes in the linked list.

examples.explanation: The original linked list has the following structure:       1 / \     2   3 \       4 \         5 The flattened linked list is: 1 -> 2 -> 3 -> 4 -> 5
The original linked list has the following structure:   1 /   2 The flattened linked list is: 1 -> 2 -> 3

hints.text: The problem involves a linked list with nodes that may have multiple children. Think about how you can traverse the list and identify nodes with multiple children.
Consider using a stack or similar data structure to keep track of nodes that need to be processed. You may need to temporarily store nodes with multiple children.
The key to solving this problem is to think about how you can iteratively take nodes with multiple children and 'flatten' them into a single-level linked list, preserving the original order of nodes.

editorial.title: Flattening of Linked List
editorial.intuition: The problem requires flattening a linked list, which can be achieved by traversing the list and appending node values in the correct order. A brute force approach would involve recursive traversal, but this can be optimized using a stack-based approach.
editorial.bruteForce: The brute force approach involves using recursive function calls to traverse the linked list, which results in a time complexity of O(n) but can lead to a stack overflow for large inputs due to the recursive call stack.
editorial.betterSolution: An iterative approach can be used to optimize the solution, reducing the risk of stack overflow.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution utilizes a stack to store nodes to be visited, allowing for efficient traversal of the linked list in O(n) time complexity and O(n) space complexity. This approach ensures that all nodes are visited in the correct order, resulting in a flattened linked list.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by demonstrating that each node is visited exactly once and that the order of visitation is correct. This can be achieved by showing that the stack-based approach traverses the linked list in a depth-first manner, ensuring that all nodes are visited in the correct order.
editorial.dryRun: A dry run of the optimal solution involves initializing the stack with the root node and then iteratively popping nodes from the stack, appending their values to the result list, and pushing their child nodes onto the stack. This process continues until the stack is empty, at which point all nodes have been visited and the linked list has been flattened.
commonMistakes: Using a recursive approach without considering the risk of stack overflow for large inputs
Failing to properly handle the order of visitation for child nodes
Not initializing the stack correctly with the root node
Not checking for empty stacks before attempting to pop nodes
Not appending node values to the result list in the correct order
